questions.question_text: A qualifying broker delegates all trust account management to an associate broker. If funds are mishandled, who bears responsibility?
distractors: The associate broker exclusively
Both brokers share responsibility equally
Neither broker if policies were in place
questions.explanation: The qualifying broker is ultimately responsible for trust account management and all brokerage operations, regardless of delegation. Supervision duties cannot be fully delegated.

questions.question_text: Which duty is a qualifying broker required to perform regarding associate broker documents?
distractors: Sign all contracts on behalf of the AB
File copies with NMREC within 5 days
Notarize all closing documents
questions.explanation: Qualifying brokers must review and approve all contracts and documents prepared by their associate brokers as part of their supervisory responsibility.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker notices an associate broker routinely fails to submit transaction documents. The QB should:
questions.correct_answer: Address the issue and enforce compliance
distractors: Ignore it if transactions close successfully
Wait until the next performance review
Report only if a client complains
questions.explanation: QBs must ensure compliance with all real estate laws and Commission rules. When an AB fails to follow procedures, the QB must address it immediately as part of supervisory duties.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker's supervisory responsibilities include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.correct_answer: Accompanying ABs to every property showing
distractors: Managing brokerage trust accounts
Reviewing contracts prepared by ABs
Ensuring compliance with Commission rules
questions.explanation: While QBs must supervise ABs, they are not required to attend every showing. Their duties focus on trust accounts, document review, compliance oversight, and record maintenance.

questions.question_text: Who is responsible for maintaining transaction records at a NM brokerage?
distractors: Each individual associate broker
The brokerage office manager
NMREC maintains all records centrally
questions.explanation: The qualifying broker is responsible for maintaining all transaction records at the brokerage. Records must be kept for 6 years and be readily accessible for NMREC inspection.

questions.question_text: An associate broker wants to advertise using only their personal name, without mentioning the brokerage. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Not permitted under NM advertising rules
distractors: Allowed if the AB pays for the advertising
Permitted for social media posts only
Acceptable if the AB includes a phone number
questions.explanation: Associate brokers must disclose their broker status and qualifying broker affiliation. All advertising must include the qualifying broker's name — personal-only ads are not permitted.

questions.question_text: An associate broker opens a separate trust account to hold a client's earnest money. Under NM law, this is:
distractors: Required for each transaction
Allowed with NMREC approval
Permitted if the QB consents in writing
questions.explanation: Associate brokers cannot maintain their own trust accounts. All client funds must be deposited into the qualifying broker's trust account.

questions.question_text: An associate broker's qualifying broker retires. The AB must:
questions.correct_answer: Affiliate with a new qualifying broker to practice
distractors: Continue practicing independently for 90 days
Apply to NMREC for temporary independent status
Automatically upgrade to qualifying broker status
questions.explanation: Associate brokers must always be affiliated with a qualifying broker to practice. When the QB retires, the AB must transfer to a new QB before conducting any further business.

questions.question_text: Which statement about associate brokers in NM is TRUE?
questions.correct_answer: They must comply with all brokerage policies
distractors: They may set their own commission rates
They can operate from a home office independently
They may supervise unlicensed assistants directly
questions.explanation: Associate brokers must comply with all policies set by their qualifying broker. The QB sets brokerage policies and the AB must follow them as a condition of affiliation.

questions.question_text: When meeting a potential client, an associate broker must disclose:
questions.correct_answer: Their broker status and QB affiliation
distractors: Their personal sales volume history
The number of ABs at their brokerage
Their commission split with the QB
questions.explanation: Associate brokers must disclose their broker status and their qualifying broker affiliation to consumers. Commission splits and sales history are not required disclosures.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker wants to operate out of a home office without registering it with NMREC. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, the office must be registered with NMREC
distractors: Yes, home offices are always exempt
Yes, if fewer than 3 ABs are affiliated
Yes, if the QB notifies NMREC within 30 days
questions.explanation: Qualifying brokers must have a physical office location registered with NMREC. Whether it is a commercial or home office, it must be properly registered with the Commission.

questions.question_text: A brokerage opens a second office in another city. What must the qualifying broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Register the branch office with NMREC
distractors: Obtain a separate QB license for that office
Hire a managing broker for the branch only
Nothing if it is within the same county
questions.explanation: Branch offices must be registered with NMREC. The qualifying broker does not need a separate license but must ensure the branch is properly registered and supervised.

questions.question_text: Where must a qualifying broker's license be displayed?
distractors: On the broker's personal vehicle
On each listed property's signage
Only on the NMREC website listing
questions.explanation: The qualifying broker's license must be displayed prominently at the registered brokerage office. This ensures the public can verify the broker's credentials.

questions.question_text: An associate broker conducts a transaction independently, without going through the brokerage. Under NM law:
questions.correct_answer: All business must go through the brokerage
distractors: This is permitted for referral fees only
This is allowed for lease transactions
This is acceptable if the AB has E&O insurance
questions.explanation: All real estate business must be conducted through the registered brokerage. ABs cannot conduct transactions independently outside their brokerage affiliation.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker retires but does not close the brokerage. The associate brokers at the firm:
questions.correct_answer: Cannot practice until a new QB takes over
distractors: May continue practicing for up to 60 days
Can operate independently during the transition
Must each obtain their own QB license first
questions.explanation: A brokerage cannot operate without a qualifying broker. If the QB retires without a successor, all ABs must stop practicing until a new QB is in place or they transfer.
